title: Merguez sausages
subtitle: North African spiced lamb sausages
excerpt: Minced lamb seasoned with harissa, garlic, cumin, coriander, and fennel, mixed vigorously and shaped into thin sausage links or flat patties, then cooked over high heat until charred and crisp outside and juicy within. The foundational North African sausage, used in tagines, couscous, and ojja.
sourceNotes: Merguez is the spiced lamb sausage of North Africa, found across Morocco, Tunisia, Algeria, and Libya. The name derives from the Berber word for sausage. The characteristic red colour comes from harissa and paprika. Commercial merguez is widely available in Britain, but homemade gives better control over heat level and spice balance.

content.text: This recipe makes skinless sausages shaped by hand, no casings required. The mixture is pressed into thin cylinder shapes (about 2 cm thick, 12 cm long) directly onto a work surface, then chilled to firm up before grilling or frying. The result is slightly rusticated compared to cased merguez but the flavour is identical.

Lamb mince with 20% fat is ideal, lean lamb will produce dry, crumbly sausages. Mix the seasoned mixture vigorously for 2 minutes so the proteins bind and the sausage holds together on the grill. Refrigerating for 30 minutes after shaping is important: the fat firms and the sausage holds its shape during cooking.

Combine the {{lamb-mince}}, {{harissa}}, {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, {{fennel-seeds}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Mix vigorously by hand for 2 minutes until the mixture is fully uniform and slightly sticky. Divide into 8 portions. Roll each on a clean surface into a sausage shape 12 cm long and 2 cm thick. Place on a tray, cover, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat a grill or heavy frying pan with {{vegetable-oil}} over high heat. Cook the merguez for 8 to 10 minutes, turning frequently, until charred outside and cooked through. Serve with flatbread, harissa, and lemon.

items.fix: Mix for the full 2 minutes until the mixture feels cohesive and slightly sticky. Do not handle them too much when turning, let the surface char and firm before moving
items.cause: Mixture not mixed long enough or too lean

items.fix: Make the sausages no thicker than 2 cm. After initial charring, reduce to medium heat and continue cooking. An internal temperature of 70°C ensures they are cooked through
items.cause: Too thick or heat too high
items.symptom: Exterior charring before interior is cooked

items.fix: Use 3 tablespoons of harissa or supplement with extra cayenne. The merguez should have distinct heat
items.cause: Commercial harissa often milder than homemade

content.text: Merguez is used across North African cooking: added to couscous royale, cooked in ojja, grilled as street food, and eaten at breakfast with eggs. In France it has become so established that it appears on standard supermarket shelves and at French barbecues alongside more familiar sausages. In Britain commercial merguez is available at halal butchers, North African shops, and increasingly in supermarkets. Making your own gives control over the heat level and freshness that commercial versions rarely match.
